questions.question: Which type of odontoid fracture is considered the "most common"?
questions.detail: Type 2 fractures occur at the base of the dens and are the most common odontoid injury.

questions.question: Which odontoid fracture is associated with the "best prognosis for healing"?
questions.detail: Type 3 fractures, which extend into the body of C2, have the best prognosis for healing.

questions.question: Type 1 odontoid fractures are rare and are characteristically related to:
choices: C. Avulsion of the alar ligament
questions.detail: A Type 1 fracture occurs at the upper part of the odontoid and is related to alar ligament avulsion.

questions.question: A "Jefferson Fracture" is an axial loading injury classically characterized by:
choices: A. Fracture of the bilateral pars of C2
B. The anterior and posterior arches of C1 blowing out laterally
C. Avulsion of the dens apex
E. Retropulsion of a bone fragment
questions.detail: In a Jefferson fracture, the C1 ring "blows out" laterally due to axial force.

questions.question: What percentage of Jefferson fractures are associated with a concomitant C2 fracture?
questions.detail: Approximately 30% of patients with a Jefferson fracture will also have a C2 fracture.

questions.question: Neurologic (cord) damage is rare in a Jefferson fracture because:
choices: A. The cord is not present at C1.
B. The force is directed into the bones and the canal widens.
C. C1 is a flexible bone.
D. The ligaments always stay intact.
E. The fractures are always stable.
questions.detail: Cord damage is rare because the arches blow outward, effectively widening the canal.

questions.question: How is "Os Odontoideum" distinguished from an acute Type 2 odontoid fracture?
choices: A. It is always associated with trauma.
B. The base of the dens is usually hypoplastic/absent.
C. It is only seen in adults.
E. It is bright on T1.
questions.detail: In Os Odontoideum, the dens base is hypoplastic, whereas it is normal in an acute fracture.

questions.question: An "Os Terminale" is a variant located at:
choices: A. The base of the dens
questions.detail: The Os Terminale is an ossicle located at the tip of the odontoid.

questions.question: "Hangman’s Fracture" characteristically involves a fracture through the:
questions.detail: A Hangman's fracture is defined as a fracture through the bilateral pars (or pedicles) of C2.

questions.question: The mechanism of injury for a Hangman's fracture is typically:
choices: B. Hyperextension (e.g., chin hitting a dashboard)
questions.detail: It is seen most commonly when the chin hits the dashboard in an MVA, causing hyperextension.

questions.question: Why is cord damage uncommon in a Hangman's fracture despite its unstable nature?
choices: A. The pars are not near the cord.
B. The acquired pars defect allows for canal widening.
C. The fractures are usually non-displaced.
E. It only occurs in children.
questions.detail: Similar to a Jefferson, the pars defect allows the canal to widen, reducing the risk of cord compression.

questions.question: A "Chance Fracture" is an unstable 3-column fracture classically associated with:
choices: A. Diving into a shallow pool
C. Direct blow to the face
questions.detail: The Chance fracture is a flexion-distraction injury classically caused by a lap-band seatbelt.

questions.question: Chance fractures are most commonly located at which level?
questions.detail: These fractures are most frequent at the upper lumbar levels and thoracolumbar junction.

questions.question: Patients with a Chance fracture have a high association with:
questions.detail: There is a high association with solid organ trauma due to the mechanism of the lap belt.

questions.question: A "Unilateral Locked Facet" is a stable injury that demonstrates which sign on axial imaging?
questions.detail: A unilateral locked facet shows the inverted hamburger sign on the dislocated side.

questions.question: "Bilateral Locked Facets" are considered highly unstable and are strongly associated with:
questions.detail: Bilateral facets involve disruption of the posterior ligaments and are strongly associated with cord injury.

questions.question: "Atlantoaxial Instability" (predental space > 5 mm) is classically associated with:
choices: B. Down Syndrome and Juvenile RA
questions.detail: The high-yield associations for atlantoaxial instability are Down syndrome and juvenile RA.

questions.question: "Rotary Subluxation" of C1–C2 in a child produces the "cock-robin" position and is often called:
questions.detail: Rotary subluxation in kids looks like torticollis; it rarely happens in adults without a fracture.

questions.question: "Spondylolysis" (pars interarticularis defect) is a stress fracture most commonly found at:
questions.detail: Pars defects are nearly always at L5 (90%).

questions.question: On oblique plain films, a pars defect is described as a:
choices: B. Eye on the Scottie Dog
C. Tail on the Scottie Dog
questions.detail: The "collar on the Scottie Dog" is the classic plain film look for spondylolysis.

questions.question: In the "Denis 3 Spinal Column Concept," instability is suggested when:
choices: A. Only the anterior column is disrupted.
B. Two or three contiguous columns are disrupted.
C. Only the posterior column is involved.
E. There is a Schmorl node.
questions.detail: Instability is suggested if all 3 or 2 contiguous columns (including the middle column) are disrupted.

questions.question: According to the Powers Ratio for occipitocervical instability, a ratio greater than what threshold is abnormal?
questions.detail: A Powers Ratio > 1.0 indicates ligamentous instability.

questions.question: "Harris Lines (Rule of 12)" states that the Basion-Dens distance should be:
questions.detail: The Basion-Dens and Basion-Posterior Axial distances should both be less than 12 mm.

questions.question: "Clay-Shoveler’s Fracture" is an avulsion of the spinous process at C7 or T1 caused by:
questions.detail: A Clay-Shoveler's fracture results from hyperflexion.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a "STABLE" spine fracture?
questions.detail: Clay-Shoveler’s and Transverse Process fractures are listed as stable injuries.

questions.question: A "Flexion Tear Drop" fracture is considered:
choices: D. Only found in the lumbar spine
questions.detail: A flexion tear drop is a highly unstable injury.

questions.question: In "Central Cord Syndrome," which clinical deficit pattern is characteristic?
choices: A. Lower extremity deficit worse than upper
B. Upper extremity deficit worse than lower
E. Only one side is affected
questions.detail: In Central Cord Syndrome, the upper extremity deficit is worse because those tracts are more medial in the cord.

questions.question: Who is the "classic" patient for Central Cord Syndrome?
choices: A. An old lady with spondylosis or a young person with an extension injury
B. A male teenager with a flexion injury
C. An infant with a breech birth
E. A patient with Down syndrome
questions.detail: This syndrome is classically seen in elderly patients with spondylosis or extension trauma.

questions.question: "Anterior Cord Syndrome" is characteristically caused by which mechanism?
questions.detail: Anterior Cord Syndrome is typically the result of a flexion injury.

questions.question: Which sensations remain INTACT in Anterior Cord Syndrome?
choices: E. Only the right side is intact
questions.detail: Motor and anterior column (pain/temp) functions are lost, but dorsal column sensations (proprioception/vibration) stay intact.

questions.question: "Brown Sequard Syndrome" results from a cord hemisection and features:
choices: B. Ipsilateral (one side) motor and contralateral sensory deficits
questions.detail: This syndrome involves motor loss on one side and sensory loss on the other.

questions.question: "Posterior Cord Syndrome" is uncommon but can be seen with hyperextension, resulting in loss of:
questions.detail: The hallmark is the loss of proprioception.

questions.question: What is the "most important factor for outcome" in a traumatic spinal cord injury?
choices: B. Presence of a hemorrhagic spinal cord injury
D. The amount of CSF leaking
questions.detail: The presence of hemorrhage in the cord is the most critical factor for a poor outcome.

questions.question: According to the "Canadian C-Spine Rule," imaging is indicated if the patient is:
choices: D. Only if they have a headache
E. Only after a fall from 1 foot
questions.detail: Age ≥ 65 is a high-risk factor requiring imaging under the Canadian C-Spine Rule.

questions.question: Under the "NEXUS Criteria," you CANNOT "clear" a cervical spine if the patient is:
questions.detail: Intoxication prevents clinical clearance of the spine.

questions.question: How many types of spinal vascular malformations (AVM/AVF) are generally described?
questions.detail: There are 4 types of spinal malformations.

questions.question: Which type of spinal vascular malformation is "by far the most common" (85%)?
questions.detail: The Type 1 Dural AVF accounts for 85% of cases.

questions.question: Type 1 Dural AVFs are typically:
choices: B. Acquired lesions seen in older patients
E. Only seen in the cervical spine
questions.detail: These are acquired and usually seen in older patients presenting with radiculomyelopathy.

questions.question: What is considered the "gold standard" for the diagnosis of a spinal dural AVF?
questions.detail: Angiography is the gold standard for diagnosis.

questions.question: On MRI, a Dural AVF characteristically shows a swollen cord with:
choices: B. Serpentine perimedullary flow voids (usually dorsal)
questions.detail: Look for T2 high signal in the cord and serpentine flow voids along the surface.

questions.question: Which spinal vascular malformation has an "Intramedullary Nidus" and is associated with HHT?
questions.detail: Type 2 features an intramedullary nidus and associations with HHT and KTS.

questions.question: "Type 3" spinal malformations are also known as the "Juvenile" type and carry:
questions.detail: The Type 3 Juvenile malformation has a terrible prognosis.

questions.question: "Type 4" Perimedullary fistulas tend to occur near which location?
questions.detail: Type 4 fistulas usually occur near the conus.

questions.question: "Foix Alajouanine Syndrome" is defined as a congestive myelopathy caused by:
choices: B. Venous hypertension from a Dural AVF
E. A ghost blowing out a back
questions.detail: This syndrome is caused by chronic venous hypertension related to an AVF.

questions.question: The classic clinical history for Foix Alajouanine Syndrome involves a 45-year-old male with:
choices: B. Lower extremity weakness and sensory deficits
questions.detail: The hallmark is progressive lower extremity weakness.

questions.question: A "Syrinx" is also known more simply as:
questions.detail: A syrinx is basically a "hole in the cord".

questions.question: "Hydromyelia" is specifically a type of syrinx that is:
choices: D. Only found in the brain
questions.detail: Hydromyelia is lined by ependyma; syringomyelia is not.

questions.question: What percentage of cord dilations are congenital and associated with conditions like Chiari?
questions.detail: 90% of these are congenital and associated with Chiari, Dandy-Walker, etc.

questions.question: The "Owl’s Eye" sign on T2 axial MRI is a pathognomonic buzzword for:
choices: B. Spinal Cord Infarct (Anterior Spinal Artery)
questions.detail: The "owl's eye" appearance in the anterior horns is classic for infarct.

questions.question: Spinal cord infarcts are most commonly caused by impairment of which vessel?
questions.detail: Impairment of the Anterior Spinal Artery is most common.

questions.question: In a cord infarct, the T2 high signal is localized to the center/anterior horns because:
choices: A. The posterior cord has no blood.
B. Gray matter is more vulnerable to ischemia.
E. It is caused by Campylobacter.
questions.detail: Gray matter has a higher metabolic rate and is more vulnerable to ischemia.

questions.question: What is the "most common" cause of a spinal cord infarct?
questions.detail: The most common cause is considered "idiopathic".

questions.question: In the real world, the "most common" cause of T2-bright cord demyelination is:
questions.detail: MS is by far the most common cause of demyelinating cord lesions.

questions.question: MS lesions in the cord are characteristically:
choices: B. Short segment (< 2 segments) and eccentric
D. Involving the full cord width
questions.detail: MS lesions are typically short segment (< 2) and involve less than half the cord width.

questions.question: "Transverse Myelitis" (TM) is a focal inflammation where lesions typically involve:
choices: A. Less than 1/3 of the cord width
B. At least 2/3 of the cross-sectional area of the cord
questions.detail: TM typically involves at least 2/3 of the cord area and causes focal enlargement.

questions.question: "Neuromyelitis Optica" (NMO or Devics) is distinguished from MS because it involves the cord and:
questions.detail: NMO is Optic Neuritis plus Transverse Myelitis.

questions.question: NMO cord lesions differ from MS because they are typically:
choices: B. Long segment and involve the full transverse diameter
questions.detail: NMO is longer and more extensive than MS.

questions.question: "Subacute Combined Degeneration" (SCD) is caused by a deficiency in:
questions.detail: SCD is caused by Vitamin B12 deficiency.

questions.question: The classic imaging sign for SCD on T2 axial MRI is the:
choices: B. Inverted V sign (bilateral symmetric dorsal column signal)
questions.detail: Bilateral dorsal column signal creates the "inverted V sign".

questions.question: Which condition in untreated AIDS patients can perfectly mimic the imaging of Vitamin B12 deficiency?
questions.detail: HIV Vacuolar Myelopathy is the main differential for the inverted V sign.

questions.question: "Arachnoiditis" is a complication of surgery or infection that involves:
choices: B. "Empty Thecal Sac Sign" or "Central Nerve Root Clumping"
questions.detail: Look for clumping or peripheral adherence (empty sac) of roots.

questions.question: For how long following spine surgery is nerve root enhancement considered "normal"?
questions.detail: Enhancement for 6 weeks post-op is considered normal.

questions.question: "Guillain-Barré Syndrome" (GBS) features which diagnostic finding on MRI?
choices: B. Enhancement of the nerve roots of the cauda equina
C. High T2 signal in the dorsal columns
questions.detail: The thing to know is enhancement of the cauda equina.

questions.question: "Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy" (CIDP) is the chronic counterpart to GBS and is described as having roots that look like:
questions.detail: The buzzword for CIDP is thickened "onion bulb" nerve roots.

questions.question: When classifying spinal cord tumors, "Astrocytoma" and "Ependymoma" are located in which compartment?
questions.detail: These are intramedullary (inside the cord) tumors.

questions.question: What is the "most common" intramedullary tumor in the pediatric population?
questions.detail: Astrocytoma is the top pediatric cord tumor.

questions.question: Spinal Astrocytomas differ from Ependymomas because they are typically:
choices: B. Eccentric and show heterogeneous enhancement
E. Only in the lumbar spine
questions.detail: Astrocytomas are usually eccentric with heterogeneous enhancement.

questions.question: What is the "most common" intramedullary mass in adults?
questions.detail: Ependymoma is the most common intramedullary mass in adults.

questions.question: The "Myxopapillary" subtype of Ependymoma is unique because it is exclusively found in the:
choices: B. Conus or Filum Terminale (Lumbar spine)
questions.detail: Myxopapillary ependymomas favor the lower cord/filum.

questions.question: On T2 MRI, a spinal ependymoma may feature a "dark cap" representing:
questions.detail: The hemosiderin cap is a classic T2 clue.

questions.question: Spinal Hemangioblastomas are associated with Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) in what percentage of cases?
questions.detail: 30% of these patients have VHL.

questions.question: What is the "most common" primary tumor to occur in the Extramedullary Intradural location?
questions.detail: Schwannoma is the most common in this compartment.

questions.question: A spinal Schwannoma characteristically has which appearance?
choices: A. Dumbbell-shaped with an intraforaminal component
questions.detail: The dumbbell shape is classic for schwannomas.

questions.question: "Plexiform Neurofibroma" is a multilevel bulky nerve enlargement that is pathognomonic for:
questions.detail: Plexiform = NF-1.

questions.question: Neurofibromas often demonstrate a "target sign" on T2, which is defined as:
choices: B. Hyperintense T2 rim with a central area of low signal
questions.detail: The target sign is a classic feature of neurofibromas.

questions.question: Spinal Meningiomas are much more common in women (70%) and typically favor which location?
questions.detail: They favor the thoracic spine in women.

questions.question: "Drop Metastases" to the spine most commonly originate from which primary brain tumor?
questions.detail: Medulloblastoma is the top "dropper".

questions.question: What is the "most common" systemic tumor to produce drop metastases (Zuckerguss) to the spine?
questions.detail: Breast cancer is the most common systemic source for drop mets.

questions.question: "Vertebral Hemangiomas" are common (seen in 10% of people) and feature which classic appearance on imaging?
questions.detail: The vertical trabeculae are diagnostic for hemangiomas.

questions.question: Vertebral hemangiomas are typically:
choices: B. T1 and T2 BRIGHT (due to fat content)
D. Purely lytic with no matrix
questions.detail: Their fat content makes them bright on T1/T2.

questions.question: "Osteoid Osteoma" in the spine favors the posterior elements and features night pain that:
questions.detail: The classic story is night pain improved with aspirin.

questions.question: An "Aneurysmal Bone Cyst" (ABC) in the spine is expansile and can demonstrate:
choices: B. Multiple fluid-fluid levels on T2
questions.detail: ABCs are known for having multiple fluid levels.

questions.question: Giant Cell Tumors of the spine are almost exclusively found in which location?
questions.detail: GCTs favor the sacrum and have no rim of sclerosis.

questions.question: What is the "most common" primary malignancy of the spine and sacrum?
questions.detail: Chordoma is the #1 primary malignancy of the spine/sacrum.

questions.question: Chordomas are characteristically:
choices: A. Very T2 bright and strictly MIDLINE
questions.detail: They are T2 bright and strictly midline.

questions.question: A "Benign Notochordal Cell Tumor" differs from a Chordoma because it is:
choices: B. Sclerotic on CT and does NOT usually enhance
questions.detail: Sclerosis and lack of enhancement favor the benign variant.

questions.question: Plasmacytoma in a vertebral body can feature a characteristic imaging appearance nicknamed:
questions.detail: "Mini Brain" = Plasmacytoma.

questions.question: Multiple spinal lesions with "low T1 signal" (darker than the disc) in an adult is highly suspicious for:
questions.detail: Low T1 signal that is darker than the disc suggests mets/cancer.

questions.question: "Vertebra Plana" (a pancake flat vertebral body) in a kid should make you first think of:
choices: B. Eosinophilic Granuloma (EG) or Neuroblastoma met
questions.detail: Vertebra plana in a kid = EG.

questions.question: "Limbus Vertebra" is a fracture mimic that is the result of:
choices: B. Herniated disc material between a non-fused apophysis and the vertebral body
questions.detail: It is caused by herniated disc material and mimics a fracture.

questions.question: "Scheuermann’s Disease" is defined by at least three levels of wedged vertebral bodies and associated:
questions.detail: The combination of wedging and Schmorl's nodes in a teenager defines this.

questions.question: A "Schmorl Node" is defined as:
choices: B. Intravertebral herniation of disc material through the endplate
questions.detail: It is an intravertebral disc herniation.

questions.question: "Modic Type 1" endplate changes represent edema and are:
choices: A. T1 Dark and T2 Bright
B. T1 Bright and T2 Bright
C. T1 Dark and T2 Dark
questions.detail: Type 1 (Edema) is T1 Dark and T2 Bright.

questions.question: "Modic Type 2" endplate changes represent fatty replacement and are:
choices: A. T1 Dark and T2 Bright
B. T1 Bright and T2 Bright
C. T1 Dark and T2 Dark
questions.detail: Type 2 (Fat) is T1 Bright and T2 Bright.

questions.question: A "Sequestration" in disc nomenclature refers to a fragment that is:
choices: A. Wider at the base than at the edges
B. Broken off and free from the disc
C. Only seen in the cervical spine
questions.detail: A sequestration is a free (broken off) disc fragment.

questions.question: A "Central or Sub-Articular" disc herniation in the lumbar spine typically smashes the:
questions.detail: Central/Sub-Articular discs hit the descending nerve (e.g., S1 nerve at L5–S1).

questions.question: A "Foraminal" disc herniation in the lumbar spine typically smashes the:
questions.detail: Foraminal discs hit the exiting nerve (e.g., L5 nerve at L5–S1).

questions.question: "Conjoined Nerve Root" is the most common developmental anomaly of the nerve root and is seen as:
choices: B. Two nerve roots arising from a single dural sleeve
C. A tumor on the root
E. High T2 signal in the dorsal columns
questions.detail: It involves two roots from one sleeve and can be a "scapegoat" in failed back surgery.

questions.question: A "Blood Patch" procedure for post-dural puncture headache involves injecting:
choices: B. 3–20 cc of the patient's own blood into the epidural space
questions.detail: The procedure uses autologous blood to seal the dural leak.

questions.question: "Orificial renal artery stenosis" in a child or teenager with hypertension is a classic vascular finding for:
questions.detail: NF-1 causes renal artery stenosis/hypertension in kids due to arterial wall dysplasia.
